title: The mole and the Avogadro constant
summary: 9701 AS Chemistry — the mole concept, Avogadro constant, molar mass, and solution concentration with PhET Molarity lab integration.
learningObjectives: Define the mole and state the value of the Avogadro constant.
Convert between mass, moles, and number of particles using n = m/Mr.
Calculate solution concentration c = n/V in mol dm⁻³.
Use the molar gas volume at rtp (24 dm³ mol⁻¹) in gas stoichiometry.

simpleExplanation.title: Chemists count in moles
simpleExplanation.summary: The mole is a counting unit — like a dozen, but 6.02 × 10²³ particles. It links the microscopic (atoms) to the macroscopic (grams you can weigh).
steps: One mole of any substance contains 6.02 × 10²³ specified particles (atoms, molecules, ions).
Molar mass in g mol⁻¹ numerically equals Mr — so 1 mol of CO₂ has mass 44 g.
Concentration c = n/V links moles dissolved to solution volume in dm³.
At rtp, 1 mol of any ideal gas occupies about 24 dm³ — essential for gas equations.

flashcards.front: n = m / Mr rearranged for mass?

sections.content: Stoichiometry dominates **9701 AS Papers 1 and 2**. If the mole concept is shaky, every calculation that follows — titration, gas volume, limiting reagent — will cost marks. The **PhET Molarity** sim below connects moles to solutions you prepare in the lab.
n = m/Mr | n = cV (V in dm³) | n = volume of gas (dm³) / 24 at rtp | N = n × L

sections.question: Calculate the mass of NaOH needed to prepare 250 cm³ of 0.100 mol dm⁻³ solution. (Mr NaOH = 40.0)

items: Always convert cm³ to dm³ before using c = n/V
State units in every line — examiners deduct for missing mol, g, dm³
Distinguish empirical formula mass from molecular formula mass
Significant figures: match data in the question unless told otherwise

sections.content: Write **n = m/Mr** first even when the answer seems obvious. That M1 line appears in almost every mark scheme for calculation questions.
